english: medicinal herb of an astrin-gent taste.
Lex. w.e.: śa-bai ya-dor (sha ba'i ya dor): Sch. explains: the growth of a new branch on a stag's horn.
C. (pronounced *ghande* (*ghande*)) how?

= gaṅ-na (gang na), where, used interr. and correl., frq.; gá-na-ba (ga na ba) and gaṅ-na-ba (gang na ba) the same as a sbst., the whereabouts of a person, his place of residence: rgyál-po gá-na-bar (rgyal po ga na bar), (or gá-na-ba der (ga na ba der), gá-na dŭg-par (ga na dug par), gá-na bźŭgs-par (ga na bzhugs par)) soṅ (song) he went where the king was Dzl., frq. -- *ga-na-méd* (*ga na med*) W. absolutely, at all events, *ga-na-méd kal gos* (*ga na med kal gos*) it must be sent by all means; *ga-na-méd lóg-te taṅ yin* (*ga na med log te tang yin*) I shall give it back at all events (B. ċis-kyaṅ (cis kyang)).
camphor Med.
n. of a medicine Med.
how, how much, how many how long, interr. and correl.; as much as, e.g. as much as you like (you may take) col.
an eruption of the skin W.
C. how much, *rin gha-tsö* (*rin gha tsö*) what is the price?

wylie: ga zha, ga gzha', ga sha
english: a jest, joke, laughter, gá-źa daṅ rtséd-mo rtse (ga zha dang rtsed mo rtse) Pth. they jest and play; also adj.: inclined to jesting, *di-riṅ gá-śa mi dug* (*di ring ga sha mi dug*) he is not in a good humour, in good spirits, to-day W.
W. how, interr. and correl.
W. squinting.
= gá źa (ga zha) W.; *ga-ri mi rag* (*ga ri mi rag*) I am in low spirits, dejected.
= gaṅ-du (gang du) 1. whither, which way, to which place, whereto. -- 2. where, interr. and correl.
the Garuda-bird. v. kyuṅ (khyung).
1. where is? B. and col. -- 2. Ld. a species of Lathyrus.

for gaṅ-la (gang la), ċi-la (ci la) C.: *ghá-la tén-ne ne' jhuṅ* (*gha la ten ne ne' jhung*) owing to what, or from what cause did the disease arise? *ghá-la ṗen* (*gha la phen*) to what does this serve, of what use is this? Sch.; whither, to what place? *ghá-la ḍó-ghi yim-pa* (*gha la do ghi yim pa*) Ü, where are you going to? -- gá-la-ba (ga la ba) = gá-na-ba (ga na ba).

C. slowly, softly, gently, gen. in a good sense, opp. to every thing turbulent; therefore in exchanging compliments on meeting or parting: *_o-ná ghá-le ku źu naṅ* (*'o na gha le ku zhu nang*) (perh. to be spelled sku bźugs snaṅ (sku bzhugs snang)) says the person that has paid a visit, *_o-ná ghá-le ṗeb* (*'o na gha le pheb*) he that received the visit, when taking leave of each other, both phrases implying about the same as our farewell! good-bye! Cf. snaṅ-ba (snang ba).
W. squinting.

1. v. ga-źa (ga zha). -- 2. girth or rope slung across breast and shoulder in order to draw or carry anything; also dog-harness; also the bandoleer or shoulder-belt, worn as a badge of dignity by constables and the like officers; sobriquet for the rope of meditation, v. sgom-tág (sgom thag).
C. *gha-śé* (*gha she*), some, part; *bhú-mo yaṅ gha-śé ċo jhê-pa yin* (*bhu mo yang gha she co jhe pa yin*) even girls, in part, take to religion (become nuns).
v. śed (shed).
glass-beads, glass-pearls Sch.
instead of تعزير punishment Ld.
1. silver in bars, ingots, small pieces etc., uncoined W. -- 2. wad, wadding (for loading muskets) W. -- 3. C.: = bya-gág (bya gag), gag-tsé (gag tse) a water-fowl.
Med., a swelling in the throat Cs.; gag-lhóg (gag lhog) id. (?)

I. interr. pron. 1. who? which? B., C., W.; when used adjectively, it generally follows its sbst. (so at least in good language), and if preceding it, it stands in the genit. case: phyogs gaṅ (phyogs gang) which
